summary:
Hajji Firuz Tepe is a Neolithic site in the Urmia Basin of northwestern Iran, excavated by Mary Voigt and Robert Dyson in the 1960s and dating to approximately 6000 BCE. The site is internationally recognized for yielding what is widely regarded as the earliest chemical evidence of wine production: a large ceramic jar excavated from a Neolithic kitchen contained residues of tartaric acid along with resin from the terebinth tree, consistent with the storage of a resinated wine.

This finding, analyzed by Patrick McGovern of the University of Pennsylvania Museum, established Hajji Firuz Tepe as a landmark site in the archaeology of fermented beverages and contributed to broader understanding of the early origins of wine-making in the Zagros foothills — the same region where wild grape vines (Vitis sylvestris) grow naturally. The proximity of the site to wild grape populations makes it geographically plausible as an early center of wine production, and the discovery is consistent with evidence from other sites in the region suggesting that the [[event:1414|earliest wine production]] was centered in the southern Caucasus and northern Zagros zone.

Beyond its wine evidence, Hajji Firuz Tepe was a typical early Neolithic farming community with mud-brick architecture, evidence of cereal cultivation, and herded livestock. The site fits into the broader landscape of early farming communities scattered across the highland zones flanking Mesopotamia, each of which contributed to the gradual development of the agricultural and craft traditions that would eventually give rise to the complex societies of the ancient Near East.
